It is a scholarly reference index listing Hebrew words and phrases drawn from kabbalistic and biblical sources that appear throughout Zorzi's text, each entry accompanied by precise citations to the book, cantus, and chapter locations within the three-book structure of De Harmonia Mundi. The index covers both individual Hebrew terms and compound phrases, including divine names, sefirot terminology, and Kabbalistic technical vocabulary. The entries reference passages distributed across all three books, with particularly dense citation clusters in Books 1 and 2. Due to font-encoding degradation in the digital file, the Hebrew characters are rendered as Unicode codepoint substitutions rather than legible script, though the citation apparatus remains intact and structurally informative.

heading: INDICE DELLE VOCI EBRAICHE 2867
card_summary: This chunk contains the Hebrew word index (Indice delle Voci Ebraiche) from Saverio Campanini's 2010 Bompiani critical edition of Francesco Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, spanning pages 3031-3034 and continuing across two index pages (2867-2868). The index lists Hebrew terms appearing throughout the three-book structure of the work, each entry followed by precise locators in the format Book.Chapter.Section. Entries span the full range of the Hebrew alphabet, with terms drawn from Kabbalistic, biblical, and Talmudic vocabulary. The index serves as a tool for tracing Zorzi's engagement with Hebrew sources across all three cantica of the Harmonia. The text is heavily affected by font-encoding corruption (CID character substitution), rendering many of the actual Hebrew words unreadable in the digital file, though the citation structure remains intact.

heading: NDICE DELLE VOCI GRECHE
card_summary: Chunk 157 constitutes the back-matter index apparatus of the Campanini edition of De Harmonia Mundi, covering two distinct indices: an 'Indice delle voci greche' (index of Greek terms) and a substantial portion of the 'Indice dei passi biblici' (index of biblical passages). The Greek index presents individual Greek terms encoded in the source PDF, each accompanied by their location references in the tripartite structure of the work (book, cantus, chapter). The biblical index provides exhaustive verse-by-verse citations for Genesis and Exodus, and begins Leviticus, mapping every scriptural passage cited throughout De Harmonia Mundi to its location in Zorzi's text. Genesis citations range from 1:1 through 49:27, tracking Zorzi's use of patriarchal narratives — especially the blessings of Jacob (Genesis 49), the stories of Joseph, and the theophanies to Abraham — while Exodus citations concentrate heavily on the Tabernacle legislation (chapters 25–27), the Passover ritual (chapter 12), the Sinai theophany, and the divine name revealed in Exodus 3:14. No discursive argument is presented; the chunk is purely a reference instrument for locating Zorzi's scriptural exegesis across the three books of the work.

heading: INDICE DEI PASSI BIBLICI 2911
card_summary: This chunk is the Index of Biblical Passages (Indice dei Passi Biblici) from the Bompiani 2010 critical edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, spanning pages 3041–3051. It covers citations from the Pentateuch (Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy), the historical books (Joshua, Judges, 1–2 Samuel, 1–2 Kings, 1–2 Chronicles, Ezra, Nehemiah), deuterocanonical books (Tobit, Judith, Esther), Job, and the Psalms at length. Each entry maps a biblical verse to one or more loci in Zorzi's text using his three-book, chapter, and sub-chapter system (e.g., 1,7,22 = Book 1, Canticle 7, chapter 22). The Psalms section is the most densely cited, reflecting Zorzi's sustained engagement with the Psalter as a source for his harmonic theology.

heading: INDICE DEI PASSI BIBLICI 2921
card_summary: This chunk is a scholarly index of biblical passages cited throughout Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, covering books from the Psalms (Ps. 96–150) through the Wisdom literature (Proverbs, Qohelet, Song of Songs, Wisdom of Solomon, Sirach) and continuing through the Major and Minor Prophets (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Baruch, Ezekiel, Daniel, Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah). Each verse reference is keyed to the precise location in De Harmonia Mundi using the tripartite structure of Tone, Canticle, and Chapter (e.g., 1,7,19 = Tonus I, Canticum 7, Chapter 19). Heavily cited passages include Isaiah 53 (the Suffering Servant), Psalm 103, Song of Songs chapters 1–8, and Ezekiel's vision of the chariot (chapters 1 and 10), reflecting the Christological, cosmological, and Kabbalistic priorities of Zorzi's argument.

heading: INDICE DEI PASSI BIBLICI 2931
card_summary: This chunk is a portion of the Index of Biblical Passages (Indice dei Passi Biblici) from Campanini's 2010 Bompiani edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, covering pp. 3061-3069. It runs from the late Old Testament minor prophets (Jonah, Micah, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi, and the Maccabees) through all four canonical Gospels, specifically Matthew, Mark, and the beginning of Luke. Each entry cites the biblical verse and then lists the locations in Zorzi's text where that passage is invoked or interpreted, keyed to the work's internal book-chapter-section system. The Gospel of Matthew receives the most extensive treatment, spanning the Sermon on the Mount, parables, eschatological discourse, Passion narrative, and resurrection. The density of cross-references demonstrates how comprehensively Zorzi wove New Testament proof-texts through his Christian Kabbalist synthesis.

heading: INDICE DEI PASSI BIBLICI 2939
card_summary: This chunk is the Index of Biblical Passages (Indice dei Passi Biblici) from Campanini's 2010 Bompiani edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, covering pages 3069-3077 and spanning page numbers 2939-2946 in the index sequence. The entries proceed through the concluding chapters of Luke's Gospel, the entire Gospel of John (chapter by chapter, verse by verse), the Acts of the Apostles, and the Letter to the Romans, concluding partway through 1 Corinthians. Each entry cites chapter and verse of the biblical text paired with one or more location codes in the tripartite structure of De Harmonia Mundi (e.g., 1,4,19; 2,3,15; 3,7,6), indicating every locus in Zorzi's text where that scriptural passage is cited or discussed. The index reveals that the Gospel of John is among the most densely cited scriptures in the entire work, with passages such as John 1,3; 6,51; 10,9; 14,6; and 15,5 accumulating the greatest number of cross-references across all three canticles.

heading: INDICE DEI PASSI BIBLICI 2947
card_summary: This chunk is the Index of Biblical Passages (Indice dei Passi Biblici) for Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, covering New Testament texts from 1 Corinthians through Revelation. The index maps chapter-and-verse references to the specific loci within the three-book structure of the work, using Zorzi's internal citation system (book, tone, and chapter numbers). Epistles covered include 1 and 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 and 2 Thessalonians, 1 and 2 Timothy, Hebrews, James, 1 and 2 Peter, 1 and 3 John, Jude, and the Apocalypse. The Epistle to the Hebrews and the Apocalypse receive the densest citation networks, reflecting their centrality to Zorzi's Christological, angelic, and eschatological arguments. Each verse entry lists multiple cross-references within the work, revealing how extensively a single scriptural locus is woven through Zorzi's harmonizing synthesis.

heading: INDICE DEI PASSI BIBLICI 2955
card_summary: This final chunk of the Bompiani 2010 edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi contains the tail end of the Index of Biblical Passages (Indice dei Passi Biblici), covering the concluding verses of the Book of Revelation (chapters 20-22). Each verse citation is cross-referenced to the specific canticle, tone, and chapter of Zorzi's tripartite structure (e.g., 3,8,2,2 or 1,6,19). The chunk also includes a publisher's note from the Fondazione Arnone–Bellavite Pellegrini and its Centre for Science, Philosophy and Language Research (CeSPhiLaR), describing the broader editorial series 'Classici dell'Umanesimo e del Rinascimento' in which this edition appears. That series description names dozens of Renaissance and medieval thinkers — from Ficino and Pico della Mirandola to Machiavelli, Erasmus, and Paracelsus — situating Zorzi within the Platonic-Neoplatonic tradition alongside Cusanus and Giordano Bruno. The Kabbalah and Leone Ebreo are specifically named among the religious-philosophical currents the series addresses.
